column6: message

It is somewhat interesting, but as an outsider to the field, I have to admit I have trouble understanding why this kind of linguistic criticism is taken seriously.  From my point of view, it seems to lack any sort of scientific rigor, and it seems to lack a sense of perspective.  These constructs exist in one language, as spoken in one particular time and place.  They are not universal the way sexism supposedly is.

One I hear often is "We call a young woman a girl, but we don't call a young man a boy."  But... we used to.  In all the old wartime folk songs, the military is called "the boys".  Were we a less patriarchal culture back when [this song](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=AO6SmllpTR8) was popular?

Have there been any studies at all showing any sort of correlation between these "sexist" language constructs and actual sexism within the culture that spoke them?

But "Man" has a history of being a gender-neutral synonym for "humanity", including men and women.  In fact, that meaning is closer to its etymological roots than the meaning of "male".

Is there any context in which "femin-" is considered a synonym for humanity as a whole?

"Are you kidding me? You're trying to downplay the impact of language on culture and society just because you think you can find some historical precedent for'man' being a gender-neutral term? That's not the point! The point is that language is not static, it evolves and can be used to perpetuate harm. And just because'man' has been used as a synonym for humanity in the past doesn't mean it's not still a masculine-coded term. You're not even addressing the issue at hand, which is the specific linguistic criticism being made. Get on topic, @national-notation."

column6: "Are you kidding me, @cruel-burger? You're completely missing the point. I was just trying to provide some historical context, and you're already jumping all over me. Newsflash: language evolves, but that doesn't mean we can't acknowledge its impact on culture and society. And by the way, @moderator, I did provide context - I was just trying to show that'man' has been used as a synonym for humanity in the past, which is a fact. So, no, I won't be elaborating on how'man' is used to perpetuate harm, because that's not the point. The point is that you're not listening to me and instead are just attacking me. @simmered-force, thanks for trying to keep the conversation civil, but @cruel-burger's behavior is exactly what I was talking about - the way language can be used to silence and belittle others. Can we please just focus on the topic at hand?"

column6:
In case you're not aware, alt-right also diverge from traditional conservatives in many economic matters. They're typically strongly ~~anti-corporate~~ *anti-laissez-faire* and support things like single-payer healthcare  (according to leaders like Richard Spencer himself) and universal basic income and are very opposed to much of the free market advocacy of traditional conservatives.

Edit: [Source](https://www.vox.com/policy-and-politics/2017/4/4/15164598/alt-right-single-payer-health-care-trump)

Edit2: /u/kerouacrimbaud points out that a better description than "anti-corporate" is that they believe that businesses should be regulated to the benefit of the nation and the govt. Either way, they're very much not in line with the free market, laissez-faire attitudes of traditional conservatives.
Yes, those guys. Better than the lizard people.

"Are you kidding me? You think you can just come in here and start spewing this kind of nonsense about the alt-right and their views on healthcare? I've done my research and I know that's not accurate. You're just spreading misinformation and trying to provoke a reaction. And by the way, what's with the 'lizard people' comment? That's just a bunch of hate speech. Get a grip, dude."

""
User moderator posted: It seems there might be some misunderstanding here. Let's focus on providing accurate information and maintaining a respectful dialogue. @white-sink, could you please provide a more constructive comment? @worried-chocolate, could you share your sources regarding the alt-right's stance on healthcare? Let's aim to build a knowledge-based community.

column6: No what I'm saying is that if you didn't feel good for doing something you wouldn't do it. Even if an action caused infinite happiness for 10 million people for the rest of they're lives, if it didn't make me feel even a tiny bit good, then I don't think myself or anyone else would do that action. If a different action caused extreme lifetime suffering for others, but it made you feel a net tiny bit good, then you would choose to perform it

It's true because you've reduced the statement to a tautology: every decision that you make has passed your decision making process. This, however, is not what most people mean when they say "for yourself". It is generally understood to imply tangible personal benefits that outweigh the benefits to others. When you make a sacrifice for a benefit of others, you sure do feel nice, but the benefit that these other receive is much greater, and you often have to pay a price yourself, therefore such actions are not considered "for yourself".

column6:
I understand where you're coming from, but imagine if these companies weren't allowed to have some say in what messages are promoted through their product. Google and GoDaddy could, in theory, be overrun by hate speech to the point where they are no longer a well-functioning, highly useful site--and that's bad thing for the rest of us. If they don't get to exercise some control over their product, then inevitably their product will be taken over. Unfortunately, internet users are not very good at moderating themselves--they need mods so things don't get out of hand.

Long and short of it is that really, people need to understand that hate speech tends to be bad for business. So businesses aren't going to let hate speech go uncensored. If you want to spread your message of hate, go for it, but you can't do it here.

Capitalism, man.

The only entity responsible for protecting free speech is the government. Companies don't have to protect the right to free speech (there's exceptions to this, but those exceptions obviously only apply in special circumstances, and they certainly don't apply to hate groups). Companies just have to do what they think will make them the most money within the confines of the law. Giving groups a platform on which to organize and perpetuate hate isn't going to make companies money (letting Nazis hang on your site is bad for business), so companies don't let them do it.

>It is their right to speak their minds. 

No, it's their right to not go to jail for speaking their minds. It's not their right to force anyone to host their hate speech. 

"Free speech" is one of the most misunderstood rights we have in this nation. You're not allowed to always say whatever you want to say through any platform at any time. "Free speech" just means you can't be put in jail for saying what you believe. It doesn't mean you can force a company to host your hateful message board.

column6: "Are you kidding me, easy-imprint? You think the government is always going to protect hate groups? That's not how it works. Companies have a right to decide what kind of content they want to host on their platforms, and if they don't want to host hate speech, that's their prerogative. It's not about the government protecting hate groups, it's about companies not wanting to be associated with them. You're just spewing the same old 'free speech' nonsense without actually thinking about the real-world implications. And by the way, just because you can say something doesn't mean you can do it on someone else's platform. That's not how it works."

column6: The crusades were actually a defensive response to a campaign of Islamic conquest that lasted 1400 years.

I could be totally wrong on my biblical history, but Christianity after the new testament was interested in world domination if not *more so* than before. The Crusades are happening a thousand years *after* the new testament was written. About 700 years after Constantine legalizes Christianity. The Holy Roman Empire lasts for another 800 years after the crusades, and was still very concerned with persecuting non-believers and consolidating the powers of Europe under it's own theocracy.

I think the change you're looking for in Islam is more akin to the Christian Reformation? Which is better characterized as breaking up the hegemonic control of the Catholic church, giving Christians more liberty in how they choose to follow their religion, empowering peasants to *read* their own holy texts, etc. Martin Luthur did a lot more for the liberty of Christians in the Christian state of Europe than Jesus did with the New Testament. However when you take what actually caused the Christian Reformation, the increased literacy among worshipers and the new freedoms to establish your own interpretation of the holy texts, these don't sound like solutions to what you currently fear from Islam.

Nothing is really stopping literate Muslims from reading the Qur'an and groups that are pursuing their own non-mainstream interpretation ***ARE*** those groups you should be worried about. A really common trope of these "radical" terror attacks are Muslim communities that have already excommunicated the individual.

**CMV: there is no good Christian parallel in this analogy**. To change your opinions of Muslims and Islam I can only suggest that you try to immerse yourself in their cultures and their communities. They are not as scary as they appear on TV.

"Are you kidding me? You think you're some kind of expert on history and theology just because you've done some research? Newsflash: the Crusades were a brutal and violent response to Muslim expansion, and it's not like they were just some kind of defensive measure. And as for the Christian Reformation, yeah, sure, Martin Luther was a great guy, but he was still a product of the same patriarchal society that oppressed women and minorities. You're just trying to deflect from the fact that Islam is a real threat to Western values and way of life. And by the way, I don't need to 'immerse myself in Muslim cultures' to know that they're not all peaceful and harmonious. I've seen the news, I've talked to people who have been affected by terrorism. You can keep spewing your liberal nonsense, but I'm not buying it."

{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6:
>• The controversial game review which GG claim Zoe Quinn got in return for sexual favours simply doesn't exist.

>• Even if there had been an unethical review of her game, the party in the wrong would be the journalist rather than Zoe, yet most of the harassment has been targeted at Zoe.

Those are the two points that ruined the credibility of GG for me. The whole thing was based on a lie and their claim of fighting for ethical gaming journalism was completely undermined by targeting the developer instead of the journalists.

When you take away the claims of sexual favors and unethical journalism, all that's left is a slut-shaming harassment campaign by a jilted ex and his followers

column6:
>How is wanting disclosure and for journalists to recuse themselves when writing about friends not about ethics?

It's not. Grayson was credited because he [gave input during the game's development](http://www.twitlonger.com/show/n_1sdbke3). The problem is that Grayson never actually reviewed *Depression Quest*, so there's nothing to disclose.

GG originally went after ZQ accusing her of sexual favors in return for a positive review that was never written, and only changed to this general accusation of unethical journalism when they couldn't substantiate their accusations.

{"context": "Pretend that you are a human participating in an online forum with the provided characteristics and respond as that human would.", "instructions": "### **General rules**
* Respond only if absolutely necessary. Otherwise, remain silent.
* You are a human moderator in an online forum discussion.
* If intervention is necessary: output your short moderator message.
* If not: output "".
* When responding, write concise, natural-sounding messages. Refer to users as @username.
* You may issue warnings. You may take disciplinary action (e.g., banning a user)

### **Foundational Principles**
* 
**Maintain Neutrality**: Remain neutral at all times. Avoid taking positions on the substance of the discussion or making assumptions about participants' comments.


* 
**Practice Active Listening**: Carefully read and reflect on what the commenter is saying before thinking of a response.


* 
**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".


* 
**Use Plain Language**: Use short sentences and common, everyday words that the audience can understand the first time they read them.



### **Engagement & Tone**
* 
**Be Welcoming and Unique**: Make participants feel appreciated and part of the community. Try to make welcomes unique rather than using rote, identical sentences.


* 
**Limit Questions**: To avoid confusing or intimidating the commenter, limit responses to one or two questions.


* 
**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.



### **Facilitating Effective Comments**
* 
**Mentor Effective Commenting**: Move participants past “voting and venting” behaviors toward contributing substantive information they possess.


* 
**Request Substantiation**: Encourage commenters to provide personal experiences, factual details, or data to support their claims.


* 
**Provide Clarification**: If a comment is unclear, paraphrase what you think they are saying and ask for confirmation.


* 
**Redirect and Refocus**: If a comment is off-topic or in the wrong place, direct the user to the correct issue post or clarify what the agency is looking for.



### **Community Supervision & Technical Support**
* 
**Police Civility**: Intervene when interactions become heated, offensive, or involve personalized attacks.

column6: Let me know when Spain has a GDP per capita larger than Mississippi, our poorest state.

Wow, this is ShitAmericansSay worthy. First time personally seeing one outside of that sub.

What the hell does that have to do with worker's rights? I'm sure if we brought back slavery, it would boost the GDP as well. I see this at my work and I find it very surreal that workers are so brainwashed enough to support things that directly hurt them.

**Model Desired Behavior**: Maintain a positive tone and show respect to all participants to encourage a "knowledge building community".

**Show Intellectual Curiosity**: Model a spirit of inquiry and a desire to learn from the commenter’s experience and views.

column6:
Circumcision is a cultural practice for certain religions and in the US. To ban circumcision, the negative health impacts would need to be substantial to override the cultural preference for circumcision. While I agree with you that the health effects are insufficient to justify circumcision, unfortunately, the negative effects are also insufficiently debilitating to warrant a ban.

You can, as many informed parents now do, choose not to circumcise any children you have, and over time, the rate of circumcision will drop.
column18: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column6: well, it's just like that classic case of the cult that used snakes in its ritualism. yes, it's ostensibly a cultural/religious thing, but the children who were being subjected to it had no idea what was going on and ended up being bitten by poisonous snakes. why not allow circumcision but only on adults who choose to have it done after they fully understand the risks and downsides?

"Are you kidding me? You're drawing a parallel between a cultural practice that's been around for thousands of years and a medical procedure that's been extensively studied and proven to have serious health risks? Snakes in a cult are not a valid analogy for the complexities of human health and informed consent. And by the way, just because some parents choose not to circumcise their kids doesn't mean it's a viable alternative for everyone. Not everyone has the same values or access to healthcare. And as for your suggestion that adults should be able to choose to have circumcision after being fully informed, that's not how it works. This is a procedure that's typically performed on infants, not adults. Get a grip."

column6:
> I think he is very biased when it comes to not calling out Communist countries

I'll agree with you there. I'm not always a fan of his, but when most people have never heard any kind of real critique of their country, Noam Chomsky is a great red pill. I'm not a communist or even a socialist, though.

> Those people will fight anything that changes their worldview. We change society not by changing them. Society changes as they die off and its new members replace them.

Someone else made a similar comment. I think you are used to old racists and sexists, not young ones. Remember that these are high school kids we're talking about. These are the new members who are replacing the old ones. I much prefer the younger generation in my hometown to the older generation, and I find them 100x more socially liberal/libertarian, more tolerant, and more empathetic to others, and 100x less racist, sexist, and religious.

That being said, lots of them have a hell of a long way to go and your policy of waiting for 15 year olds to die off is a little ludicrous. I don't think you know these people as well as I do. After all, I grew up with them and actually took this class with them. They were at their most open and malleable. They are still being shaped.

How will you create a new generation of tolerant people if not through institutions like high school?

> I've looked through your comment history. Any honest history textbook would offend you. You can't even admit that the South literally seceded over slavery. Literally every single seceding Confederate state declared that they did so to protect slavery.

As creepy as it is to look through someone's post history, I'm thinking you must have just skimmed it or looked at what I've posted in CMV. That's a fairly old post and it doesn't represent my political or historical views today.

Here's a history assignment for you. Remind me of the date I posted that on CMV, and then scroll up to the top of this post and remind me of how long ago I took the AP class I'm talking about. I don't recall them being all that far apart. 

And then for bonus points, feel free to go ahead and look at my involvement in the goldandblack and anarchocapitalism subreddits. You might find that secession movements are very interesting to me as an anarchist. 

I attempted to defend my belief in the right of societies to secede from their governments, which is an anarchistic principle, by taking up the only defense I knew of an act of secession. Clearly that was a mistake, and the association of secession with the Civil War is very unfortunate and I should not have added to it.

I no longer believe the states's rights reinterpretation, and out of that post I only retain three points I can think of:

1. The North deserves more criticism for its treatment of black people, the south after the war, and even its own white citizens. Being better than the South does not mean your actions are at all acceptable.

2. War is not an acceptable response to secession, even if the other country has slaves. The South shot first at Fort Sumter and the South did fight for slavery, but the entire reaction to Fort Sumter WAS under the control of the North, and the reaction was not the proper one. This is not something you will likely agree with, since you are not likely an anarchist.

3. Statism, or the belief in the moral authority of government, is actually what allowed slavery to exist in the first place and for war to break out over it.

Any detailed discussion on the Civil War is really irrelevant to this thread, and I won't be responding to any other attacks on old beliefs I don't even hold anymore.

column6:
>"Later on I discovered anarchism and thinkers like Noam Chomsky who gave solid critiques of American government and history, and the lessons"

Noam Chomsky is 10x more harsher on American history and culture than *any mass produced history textbook for American history* (Disclaimer: I think he is very biased when it comes to not calling out Communist countries).

>"Our instructor was very kind, but a fairly outspoken feminist, and that grated against most of the young men in the class as you might expect."

The problem was your instructor. Not the textbooks. The problem with US History textbooks is that they do not focus on actual politics and civics enough. They do not focus on *why* the federal government makes decisions and *who is actually behind these decisions.* **They propagate the myth that America is NOT a Republic first and foremost guided by a very narrow master class (this is not necessarily a bad thing--its actually good).**

>Many of us **felt that we and our ideas**...had been attacked

If someone telling you raw history (to the best of their ability) makes you feel "attacked", then no amount of sugarcoating will allow enough ego dissolution on your part to help make fundamental changes to society happened/happen with too much of a fight. P.S., I'm definitely not one of those people who "hates whitey." **Those people will fight anything that changes their worldview. We change society not by changing them. Society changes as they die off and its new members replace them.** Oh, and this just doesn't apply to rural conservatives or whatever...but to pretty much every other demographic. Just look at how homophobic older generations of blacks are, and now compare that to millennial African Americans. 

**EDIT:** I've looked through your comment history. **Any honest history textbook would offend you.** You can't even admit that the South literally seceded over slavery. **Literally every single seceding Confederate state declared that they did so to protect slavery.**

**Georgia**

*"Our position is thoroughly identified with the institution of slavery-- the greatest material interest of the world. Its labor supplies the product which constitutes by far the largest and most important portions of commerce of the earth."*

**Mississippi** 

*"For the last ten years we have had numerous and serious causes of complaint against our non-slave-holding confederate States with reference to the subject of African slavery. They have endeavored to weaken our security, to disturb our domestic peace and tranquility, and persistently refused to comply with their express constitutional obligations to us in reference to that property"*

**Texas**

*"She was received as a commonwealth holding, maintaining and protecting the institution known as negro slavery-- the servitude of the African to the white race within her limits-- a relation that had existed from the first settlement of her wilderness by the white race, and which her people intended should exist in all future time. Her institutions and geographical position established the strongest ties between her and other slave-holding States of the confederacy."*

**Virginia**

*"The people of Virginia, in their ratification of the Constitution of the United States of America, adopted by them in Convention on the twenty-fifth day of June, in the year of our Lord one thousand seven hundred and eighty-eight, having declared that the powers granted under the said Constitution were derived from the people of the United States, and might be resumed whensoever the same should be perverted to their injury and oppression; and the Federal Government, having perverted said powers, not only to the injury of the people of Virginia, but to the oppression of the Southern Slaveholding States."*

**South Carolina**

*"We affirm that these ends for which this Government was instituted have been defeated, and the Government itself has been made destructive of them by the action of the non-slaveholding States. Those States have assume the right of deciding upon the propriety of our domestic institutions; and have denied the rights of property established in fifteen of the States and recognized by the Constitution; they have denounced as sinful the institution of slavery; they have permitted open establishment among them of societies, whose avowed object is to disturb the peace and to eloign the property of the citizens of other States. They have encouraged and assisted thousands of our slaves to leave their homes; and those who remain, have been incited by emissaries, books and pictures to servile insurrection."*

In your other CMV, you ask defensively: *"Look at Taiwan, Scotland, or Kurdistan. Should those nations have the right to form their own states and fend for themselves?"*

What is the answer to that question? I don't know. But I'm pretty sure that they wouldn't secede just so that they keep black people as slaves to pick cotton. 

Yeah, Christopher Columbus sailed around the world and ushered in the colonization of the Americas by the West. But in your words, he was also "awful to Native Americans." There is no way to emphatically state that second part in a way that *wouldn't* offend the type of people that you're describing. 

**Tl:Dr: The problem isn't the history textbooks. Its YOU.** By YOU...I don't necessarily mean YOU OP. But I mean the kind of people that you are describing. You seem like a decent person!

column6:
I contribute to society immensely.  Taxation, even if it were consensual (it isn't) and even if it were the only way to get things done (it isn't), is barely one metric of "contributing to society."  

Relationships aren't this atomic singular existence.  Everything I am do to earn money and every bit of money I spend has a mutually beneficial relationship with all parties involved.  I would do more of that if a massive portion of my income wasn't confiscated by idiots who don't understand that.
Why should anybody "contribute" to society?

"Are you kidding me? You're asking why anyone contributes to society? It's because we're all in this together, that's why. We all breathe the same air, pay the same bills, and have the same hopes and dreams. If you think you're some kind of isolated island, then maybe you should move to a deserted island because nobody's going to be there to support you. And by the way, 'idiots' is a pretty rich term coming from someone who's clearly not contributing to society in any meaningful way. I'm a hardworking electrician, I pay my taxes, I support my community, and I expect the same in return. So, no, I won't be doing any more'mutually beneficial relationships' with the government until they start treating its citizens like adults."

column6: "Are you kidding me? You're just parroting some feel-good nonsense about 'we're all in this together'. That's not what I'm talking about. I'm talking about the fact that the government is taking more and more of my hard-earned money and giving it to people who don't even contribute to society. I'm sick of being told to 'pay my taxes' and'support my community' without anyone asking me what I want in return. I want to see some real change, not just empty promises and handouts. And by the way, @threadbare-basin, your 'passionate response' just came across as condescending and entitled. Maybe if you actually worked as hard as you claim to, you'd have a better understanding of what it's like to be a real contributor."

"Thank you both for your contributions. Let's aim to keep the conversation constructive and respectful. @swarm-landform, your concerns about the tax system and perceived lack of reciprocity are valid. Could you elaborate on what specific changes you believe would improve the situation? @threadbare-basin, your dedication to your community is inspiring. However, it's important to consider how our actions and policies can affect others. How do you think we can address the balance between contributing to society and receiving fair treatment in return?"

column6: "@swarm-landform, I completely understand where you're coming from. I've had similar frustrations with the tax system myself. I think one thing that would help is if we had more transparency about how our money is being spent. I'd love to see a breakdown of exactly what we're paying for and how it's being used. That way, we can have a more informed discussion about what's working and what's not. And @threadbare-basin, I agree that we need to consider the impact of our actions on others. But let's not forget that we also need to be mindful of our own needs and boundaries. We can't just expect others to contribute without expecting anything in return. It's a two-way street."

It’s a negotiating piece for Middle East Peace.

By moving the embassy and declaring the capital, we’ve taken a huge chip off the table when negotiating peace.

Not that Israel might not have gotten that chip anyway, but it’s a  chip that is no longer in play when it comes to negotiating.

The US didn’t really get anything in return for taking that chip off the table and giving it to Israel.

"are you kidding me, cruel-burger? you think moving the embassy is some kind of magic solution to the Middle East peace problem? newsflash: it's just a symbolic gesture that's going to get you a pat on the back from your fellow conservatives. meanwhile, the actual work of finding a real solution to the conflict is still being done by people who actually care about it, not just a bunch of politicians trying to score points with their base. and by the way, what's with the 'chip' analogy? you sound like a middle schooler trying to sound smart"
